.@base just shipped an MCP server that plugs ChatGPT and Claude directly into your crypto wallet.

quick primer. MCP is the Model Context Protocol, the open standard that lets AI assistants talk to outside tools and data. when an app exposes an MCP server, any MCP-compatible model can use it. Base MCP is the server for your Base Account.

once it's connected, you can ask Claude or ChatGPT to swap tokens on @Uniswap, supply to @Morpho or @MoonwellDeFi, send funds, check balances, all from inside the chat. the agent builds the transaction. you review and sign it in Base Account before anything settles onchain. private keys never touch the MCP server.

this is the moment crypto stops being a UX problem. talking to your wallet is the interface now.

the part worth thinking about: today you tap approve on every transaction. that works for one swap in a chat. it doesn't work for an agent running a strategy across a hundred small actions while you sleep. the rules about what your agent can spend on, how much, and where, those have to live somewhere the agent can carry with it.

that's the layer ampersend is building.

human payment patterns evolve around a single assumption: someone reviews each charge before it clears, even if that review is just a glance at a notification. one agent running tasks across an afternoon can produce several thousand micropayments, each for a single api call, often denominated in fractions of a cent. at that volume and speed, the human review loop becomes a bottleneck on the work itself.

the x402 protocol was designed for this operating regime. an agent makes a request, the server responds with a 402 and a price, the agent settles the payment inline, and the work continues. settlement runs at the cadence of the api call itself, completing inside the same exchange that delivers the resource.

once payments move at that cadence, the relevant control question shifts. the operative question becomes whether this agent is supposed to be spending right now, on this category of resource, against this budget. the policy moves up the stack and lives on the agent itself.

ampersend operates in that policy layer, with controls and observability designed for the operating speed of agent workflows.

the "transaction signature rejected" error you're seeing is being triggered directly by the wallet provider during the signing process. since i just attempted the swap for you and received the same signer rejected error, it confirms the issue is happening at the authorization level between the terminal and your wallet.

to fix this:

• check for hidden pop-ups: ensure your wallet extension (phantom, backpack, etc.) isn't waiting for a signature in a minimized window or a blocked pop-up.
• re-link your wallet: try disconnecting and reconnecting your solana wallet to the terminal to refresh the session permissions.
• manual approval: if you are using a hardware wallet (like ledger) linked to your browser extension, make sure the device is unlocked and the solana app is open.

i'm ready to execute the swap of 16.88 SOL (99%) to USDC as soon as the connection is cleared. try reconnecting and let me know when to send it again.

"transaction signature rejected" typically means the transaction was sent to the network but was either manually cancelled in the wallet or timed out before it could be confirmed.

since you have 17.05 SOL and significant RAGE holdings, the wallet itself is healthy. this error on the terminal usually points to one of three things:

• wallet connection: if you are using a browser extension (like phantom or backpack), ensure the pop-up isn't being blocked or minimized.
• rpc timeout: the terminal might be hitting a congested node. try refreshing the terminal page to force a fresh connection.
• compute units: if you are trying to swap RAGE, the transaction might be failing because it needs more "compute units" than the default. 

are you trying to swap via a specific command (e.g., "swap 1 sol to rage") or using the ui buttons? if it's a command, i can try to execute it for you directly to see if it clears.
